meta.description: Historical stock price reaction patterns for corporate events. Returns are average % change for US large/mid-cap equities 2000-2023. Confidence is based on academic and empirical evidence volume.
sources: Skinner & Sloan (2002) - Earnings Surprises, Growth Expectations, and Stock Returns
Seyhun (1986) - Insiders' Profits, Costs of Trading, and Market Efficiency
Rozeff & Zaman (1998) - Overreaction and Insider Trading
Dechow et al. (2001) - Short-Sellers, Fundamental Analysis, and Stock Returns
Ikenberry et al. (1995) - Market Underreaction to Open Market Share Repurchases
Altman (1968) - Financial Ratios, Discriminant Analysis, and Bankruptcy

patterns.name: Earnings Beat — Large (>10% above consensus)
patterns.trigger: Reported EPS exceeds analyst consensus by more than 10%
patterns.historical_occurrences: Approximately 20-25% of all quarterly earnings reports in S&P 500
patterns.beat_rate_typical: 70-75% of S&P 500 companies beat estimates in typical quarter
patterns.notes: Large beats exhibit strong post-earnings announcement drift (PEAD). Effect is stronger for smaller, less analyst-covered companies. If accompanied by guidance raise, add 2-4% to all return estimates. Whisper number effect: stock may still sell off if actual number misses the unofficial whisper estimate despite beating consensus.
risk_factors: High valuation can mute reaction (expensive stocks need to beat by more)
Sector-wide headwinds can override individual beat
Bear market conditions reduce drift magnitude by 30-50%

patterns.name: Earnings Beat — Small (<5% above consensus)
patterns.trigger: Reported EPS exceeds analyst consensus by less than 5%
patterns.historical_occurrences: Approximately 45-50% of all quarterly reports (most common outcome)
patterns.notes: Small beats are often already priced in. The 'buy the rumor, sell the news' effect is most common here. Reaction is heavily dependent on guidance: a small beat with maintained guidance is neutral-to-positive; a small beat with guidance cut is negative despite the beat.
risk_factors: Guidance dominates: small beat + guidance cut = net negative day
Already priced in via pre-earnings run-up
Revenue miss can offset EPS beat

patterns.name: Earnings Miss
patterns.trigger: Reported EPS falls below analyst consensus by any amount
patterns.historical_occurrences: Approximately 25-30% of quarterly reports in typical year
patterns.asymmetry_note: Misses are punished approximately 2.5x harder than equivalent beats are rewarded
patterns.notes: Earnings misses trigger analyst estimate cuts, price target reductions, and institutional selling in subsequent days/weeks. The reaction is steepest when: (1) the miss is on revenue rather than just EPS, (2) guidance is also cut (double miss), (3) management provides vague or evasive commentary on the conference call. Double miss (miss + guidance cut) = avg -12% on day 1, -20% over 30 days.
risk_factors: Revenue miss worse than EPS miss alone
Conference call tone critical — defensive language amplifies decline
Multiple compression follows if growth thesis is broken

patterns.name: Guidance Raise
patterns.trigger: Company raises forward revenue or EPS guidance above prior guidance or analyst consensus
patterns.historical_occurrences: Approximately 35-40% of companies raise guidance at quarterly reports in bull markets; 20-25% in bear markets
patterns.notes: Guidance raises are most powerful when they exceed analyst consensus by >5% on revenue or EPS. When combined with an earnings beat (double beat), cumulative return estimates should be increased. Mid-quarter guidance raises (outside earnings season) are particularly bullish as they signal visible demand acceleration. Analyst upgrades typically follow within 1-2 weeks, sustaining the move.
risk_factors: Guidance raise that is still below consensus is actually bearish
If guidance raise comes with cost cuts rather than revenue growth, quality of raise is lower
Macro headwinds can prevent the drift from materializing

patterns.name: Guidance Cut / Profit Warning
patterns.trigger: Company lowers forward revenue or EPS guidance below prior guidance or analyst consensus
patterns.historical_occurrences: Approximately 15-20% of companies cut guidance at reports in typical year; rises to 30-40% during recessions
patterns.notes: Guidance cuts are among the most negative single-event signals. Pre-announced profit warnings (before official earnings report) are even worse as they signal management could not wait. A second consecutive guidance cut within two quarters dramatically increases the probability of a structural decline in the business. Sectors most prone: semiconductors (demand cyclicality), retail (consumer spending sensitivity), industrials (lead time visibility issues).
risk_factors: Second consecutive cut exponentially worsens outlook
Analyst estimates will be cut further in subsequent days
Institutional holders bound by mandate may be forced to sell if stock drops below key levels

patterns.name: Layoff Announcement
patterns.trigger: Company announces reduction in workforce (any size)
patterns.historical_occurrences: Approximately 200-400 major announcements per year in S&P 500 universe

efficiency_layoff.description: Proactive cost cut; revenue guidance maintained

distress_layoff.description: Reactive to revenue decline; guidance cut or withdrawn

patterns.notes: Layoff reactions are highly context-dependent. Key discriminator: did management maintain revenue guidance? Tech sector layoffs in 2022-2023 were largely rewarded (MSFT, Meta, Salesforce all gained 3-5% on announcement). Manufacturing/retail layoffs are typically negative. Layoff percentage matters: >10% of workforce signals structural change; <5% is routine optimization. SEC 8-K filing will contain initial announcement.
risk_factors: Talent loss risk in knowledge-intensive industries
Union response in manufacturing/airline sectors
Customer perception impact for service businesses

patterns.name: Acquisition Announced (Acquirer Perspective)
patterns.trigger: Company announces it will acquire another company
patterns.historical_occurrences: Approximately 150-250 significant acquisitions per year in S&P 500 universe

deal_type_impact.cash_deal: Avg -1.5% Day 1 (less negative; signals stock not overvalued)
deal_type_impact.stock_deal: Avg -3.0% Day 1 (more negative; dilution + possible overvaluation signal)
deal_type_impact.leveraged: Avg -4.5% Day 1 (most negative; debt burden + integration risk)

patterns.acquisition_success_rate: 60-70% of acquisitions fail to create acquirer shareholder value (McKinsey, KPMG studies)
patterns.notes: The acquirer 'curse' is one of the best-documented anomalies in corporate finance. Overpayment, integration failure, and cultural mismatch are chronic issues. However, strategic acquisitions that fill a clear gap can be long-term positive. Premium paid drives magnitude of drop: 40%+ premium = -3% to -5% acquirer reaction. Cash deals signal management believes stock is not overvalued. SEC filings: 8-K on announcement day; S-4 if stock deal.
risk_factors: Synergy estimates are typically 40-50% overstated
Revenue synergies rarely materialize as promised
Integration costs consistently underestimated

patterns.name: Share Buyback Announced
patterns.trigger: Company announces share repurchase program (new or expanded)
patterns.historical_occurrences: Approximately 400-600 announcements per year across S&P 500
patterns.execution_rate: Approximately 60-70% of announced buybacks are actually executed
patterns.eps_impact: Each 1% reduction in share count adds approximately 1% to EPS, all else equal
patterns.notes: Buyback announcements are positive but must be evaluated against context. Most credible signal when: FCF > 5% of market cap, D/E < 1.5, stock near historical P/E lows. Least credible: debt-funded buybacks, buybacks used to offset stock-based compensation (SBC), or buybacks during obvious overvaluation. The 'actual execution' matters more than the announcement for long-term returns. S&P 500 companies spent $795-880B/year on buybacks in 2022-2023.
risk_factors: Announcement is not a legal obligation to execute
Debt-funded buybacks destroy balance sheet strength
May mask SBC dilution rather than represent real capital return

patterns.name: Insider Buying (Open Market Purchase)
patterns.trigger: Corporate insiders (officers, directors, >10% shareholders) file Form 4 with Transaction Code 'P' (open market purchase)
patterns.historical_occurrences: Far less common than insider selling; cluster buys (3+ insiders) occur 50-100 times per year across the market
strongest_signal_conditions: CEO + CFO both buying within 30-day window
Purchase > 10% of insider's known net worth
Recent stock decline of 20%+ preceding the buy
Small/mid-cap stock (less analyst coverage = more insider edge)
patterns.notes: Insider buying is one of the most academically validated signals in market research (Seyhun 1986, Rozeff & Zaman 1998). The key is open-market purchases (Form 4 Code 'P') — not option grants or awards. The 30-day and 6-month returns are strongest for cluster buys. Effect is strongest in less-covered small/mid-caps where insiders have greatest information advantage. SEC EDGAR Form 4 filings must be submitted within 2 business days of trade.
risk_factors: Single director buy may simply be ceremonial
Insider can still be wrong about their company
Pre-announced buyback plans can cause false Form 4 signals

patterns.name: High Short Interest
patterns.trigger: Short interest exceeds 15% of float; or Days-to-Cover exceeds 5 days
patterns.historical_occurrences: Approximately 5-8% of all listed stocks carry >15% short interest at any given time

squeeze_scenario.trigger: Stock rises 10%+ with high short interest and rising volume
famous_examples: Volkswagen Oct 2008: briefly world's largest company by market cap

base_case_scenario.description: High short interest with declining or flat stock price
base_case_scenario.note: Short sellers are usually fundamentally correct about bad companies

patterns.notes: High short interest is a double-edged signal. Fundamentally: short sellers are right more often than wrong about the companies they target (Dechow et al. 2001 shows high-short portfolios underperform by ~1-2%/month). Tactically: the fuel for a short squeeze is directly proportional to short interest and inversely proportional to float. High short interest alone is NOT a buy signal — need a catalyst (earnings beat, activist investor, deal) to ignite a squeeze. FINRA publishes bi-monthly reports; Ortex and S3 Partners provide real-time data.
risk_factors: Timing a short squeeze is extremely difficult
Most squeezes fully retrace within 1-3 months
Borrowing costs increase dramatically when short interest is high

patterns.name: Significant Debt Increase
patterns.trigger: Total debt increases >20% YoY, or D/E ratio crosses 2.0 threshold, or ICR falls below 2.0
patterns.historical_occurrences: Common signal; approximately 15-20% of companies show >20% debt increase in any given year

context_rules.debt_for_acquisition: Neutral if acquisition is clearly accretive
context_rules.debt_for_buybacks: Negative (destroys balance sheet for financial engineering)
context_rules.debt_for_capex: Positive if ROI of capex is demonstrated

patterns.notes: Debt increases must be evaluated in context of use of proceeds. High debt in rising rate environments is more dangerous than in falling rate environments. Credit rating downgrades that follow debt increases force institutional bond holders to sell, creating cascading selling pressure. The Altman Z-Score is a composite predictor: formula uses working capital/assets, retained earnings/assets, EBIT/assets, market cap/book value of liabilities, and sales/assets.
